context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on the spider diagram for my city case study tbh. idk what i’m supposed to put for the 5 pathways.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the course framework, not the city details. What are the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation listed in your course materials? 

Once you name them, we can sort your city’s actions under each one. What city did you choose, and can you name just 1 climate-related policy, project, or problem you already found?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief, stay focused on the assigned spider diagram, and use guided discovery rather than filling in the content for the student. To move toward the solution, I will ask the student to identify what they already know about their city and connect that to the 5 pathways, instead of supplying entries directly. This follows the rule to use the Socratic method, avoid giving answers, and keep the interaction on the assignment.

exchanges.student: ngl i don’t really remember the 5 pathways rn, can u just explain the whole framework first? my city is jakarta and i found flooding i think.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t outsource the framework—you need to retrieve it from the course. Open the module page and list the 5 pathway names first.

Since you already have Jakarta + flooding, here’s the thinking move: for each pathway, ask, “How does flooding connect to this category in the city?”

Send me the 5 names exactly as your course gives them, and we’ll sort Jakarta evidence under them one by one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a full framework lecture or complete the assignment content. Instead, I will prompt recall from the course materials and use the student’s known example—flooding in Jakarta—to anchor the categories. This keeps the help minimal, Socratic, and directed toward completing the spider diagram without giving the filled-in answer.

exchanges.student: i’m still lost tbh, the module wording is confusing and i keep mixing them up. that didn’t help much—can u just give me a simple version of all 5 so i can match jakarta stuff after?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—a simple version is:

1. Energy/materials: how the city uses resources and infrastructure.
2. Form/land use: how the city is physically built and organized.
3. Governance: who makes decisions and how policies get carried out.
4. Economy/finance: how money, jobs, and investment shape action.
5. Behavior/justice: how people live, who is affected, and whether responses are fair.

Now do the thinking: flooding in Jakarta fits most obviously under which 1 or 2 of those first, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused and explicitly asks for a simpler explanation of the framework. To support without doing the assignment, I should give a high-level, non-city-specific paraphrase of the five pathways, avoiding any Jakarta-specific entries or wording the student can copy. I will keep it bite-sized and then immediately push the student back into active reasoning by asking them to sort one example. This remains within the assignment theme, uses guided discovery, and does not provide a completed solution.

exchanges.student: maybe form/land use bc ppl built in risky areas, and governance bc idk the city didn’t manage it well? also trying a diff angle: can u tell me what usually goes in each section bc i’m not sure how specific to be.
exchanges.tutor:
That direction is better. But “didn’t manage it well” is too vague—your diagram needs observable things, not frustration.

A good rule: each pathway section should include 1) a city condition/problem, 2) a policy/project/strategy, and 3) key actors if you know them.

So for Jakarta, what is one concrete land-use fact or planning decision tied to flooding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's emerging reasoning without validating unsupported claims too strongly, then ask a focused question that helps them determine the right level of specificity for each spider diagram section. I will not provide filled content for Jakarta. Instead, I will give a generic structure for what belongs in each pathway section—issues, actors, policies, projects, and examples—so the student can populate it themselves. This keeps the response bite-sized, assignment-focused, and Socratic.

exchanges.student: idk the exact fact rn, just that flooding is bad and there are seawalls maybe. can u give me like a full example spider diagram entry so i can copy the format?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t give you a copyable entry. You need to build it.

Use this format for each pathway: issue/condition → action/response → actor.
Example of the structure only: “[problem] → [policy/project] → [who is involved].”

Try it now with your own material: where would “seawalls” go first, and what problem is that responding to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asked for a full example they could copy, which would violate the rule against providing complete or partial solutions and writing for the student. I should refuse that directly, set a boundary, and instead provide only a minimal structural template they must fill themselves. To keep it Socratic and move toward completion, I will ask for one concrete item they already mentioned and have them place it in a pathway with a reason.

exchanges.student: probably form/land use or maybe governance? like coastal flooding → seawalls → city gov maybe. ngl i still want a broader explanation bc i’m not seeing how all the sections connect.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re over-fixating on labels. Bigger picture: the spider diagram shows that one climate issue touches multiple pathways at once.

For flooding: the built environment, decisions by government, funding, and impacts on residents can all connect to the same issue.

So instead of hunting for the “perfect” section, map one issue across 3 pathways. For Jakarta flooding, what would you put under: 1) physical city form, 2) governance, 3) people/equity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same concept, so I should zoom out to the bigger picture of the assignment and explain how the sections relate without filling the diagram. I will connect pathways conceptually, affirm their partial reasoning, and then ask them to integrate by drafting a simple multi-pathway map for one issue. This follows the rule to address redundancy by moving to integration and keeps the response concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student: ok maybe physical form = seawalls/drainage, governance = city policy or agencies, people/equity = poorer areas get hit harder? i’m still kinda guessing tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable draft. Now stop guessing and test it with evidence.

Pick just one of those 3 pathway ideas and find: 1 concrete source, 1 specific action or condition, and 1 actor.

Which pathway do you want to verify first—physical form, governance, or equity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a useful attempt, so I should acknowledge that progress while pushing them to replace guesses with evidence. To follow the Socratic method and avoid giving answers, I will ask them to verify one item per pathway with a source and frame the next step as evidence collection for the spider diagram. This stays on the assignment, remains bite-sized, and helps them move from vague categories to concrete entries.

exchanges.student: maybe governance, but tbh can u just tell me what counts as an actor here bc i keep blanking.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. An actor is any person, group, or institution that influences the climate response.

For this assignment, think in categories: city government, national government, agencies, utilities, private developers, NGOs, community groups, residents, international funders.

Now apply it: for Jakarta flooding governance, which 2 actor categories seem most likely to matter first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a conceptual clarification that can be answered without doing the assignment for them. I should define 'actor' in simple terms, give non-exhaustive categories rather than city-specific content, and immediately redirect them to apply the concept to Jakarta governance. This preserves guided discovery, avoids writing their submission, and keeps the response concise.

exchanges.student: city government and maybe national government i guess. also that angle sorta worked, but i’m still fuzzy on how detailed this needs to be.
exchanges.tutor:
Keep it lean. For each pathway, aim for 2–4 short items, not a full paragraph: a problem/condition, a response, and maybe actors or a note on impacts.

Enough detail means someone can see why that item belongs there. Not enough detail is just a label like “government.”

Test yourself: can you write one governance item for Jakarta as “condition/problem → response → actor” using only keywords?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now understands actors but is uncertain about the expected level of detail. I should clarify scope in practical terms tied to the spider diagram format, without supplying city-specific content or writing for them. To keep it Socratic and move toward completion, I will give a minimal standard for sufficiency and ask them to test it on one pathway.

exchanges.student: maybe: flooding risk/inadequate drainage → flood control policy or seawall project → city gov + national agencies. is that enough or still too vague?
exchanges.tutor:
Close, but still a bit vague. The structure is good; the weak part is “policy” because it could mean anything.

Ask yourself: can you replace one generic term with something identifiable—a specific project, agency, or planning action?

Try revising just the middle part. What exact response did Jakarta use: drainage upgrades, seawall construction, relocation, zoning, emergency management, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not approve it as finished or rewrite it for the student. I will evaluate using my own judgment, note what is strong and what is vague, and ask a targeted revision question that moves them toward specificity. This stays bite-sized, Socratic, and focused on the spider diagram.
